content:
- INITIAL OBSERVATIONS
A short review and lecture on past week's price action, focusing on the Dollar Index (DXY) weekly chart.
- Price came down below relative equal lows, where sell side liquidity was taken a couple of weeks ago.
- Markets are drawn up; initial interest was in discount wicks' consequent encroachment (midpoint of each
wick).
- Price traveled higher, noting a lack of imbalance or inefficiency in that move.
- Interest now resides in the buy side liquidity above these relative equal highs, anticipating price will reach
it.

- PRICE ACTION AND SENTIMENT
Analyzing the daily chart inside the weekly decline that took out the sell side.
- A rally occurred into a buy side inefficiency, then traded down.
- Price rose, reusing an old Fair Value Gap (FVG), accumulating new long positions.
- It traded up into a Fair Value Sell Side and a Buy Side Imbalance (BISI).
- These are typical highs; the idea is for price to trade up into that area, but this changes if current lows are
lost, leading to a move down to the old sell side level.
- Current sentiment on the dollar is bullish, expecting weakness in indices and continuation lower in Forex.

- ORDER BLOCKS AND LIQUIDITY
Reviewing the DXY four-hour chart, working with the old daily Fair Value Gap.
- Price traded up into two down-closed candles, forming a bullish order block.
- A small 'buy side imbalance sell side inefficiency' (BISI) exists, not noted on the chart for cleanliness,
which would trade down into this area.
- We have an order block and Fair Value Gap, where price traded up into, falling just short of the very high
of the imbalance (defined on the daily chart).
- Even using the inefficiency shown, price didn't get to that either.
- A small measure of buy side liquidity was engaged on Friday, but continuation on the upside is expected.

- CONSEQUENT ENCROACHMENT AND RALLY
Examining the DXY one-hour chart for more detail.
- The buy side inefficiency and order block from the four-hour chart are visible.
- Price formed one, two, three, four, five lower lows, digging into the consequent encroachment (midpoint
between an hourly candle's low and another's high).
- Price traded into this consequent encroachment twice, then rallied aggressively.
- It reached for buy side liquidity, almost completely closing and repricing to the high of the shaded
inefficiency.

- VOLUME IMBALANCE AND BEARISH OUTLOOK
Moving to the EUR/USD weekly chart.
- Previous market reviews (July, May-June) highlighted a volume imbalance as a draw on liquidity, meaning
price would gravitate up into it, which it did.
- Observe the reaction: the market turned on a dime, trading lower back into the old weekly inversion Fair
Value Gap.
- This is not a cause for excitement as a buyer; the interest is whether price gets below the weekly
inversion Fair Value Gap (shaded area).
- If it treats the inversion FVG as resistance, it would dig into the sell side and reprice to a discount weekly
Fair Value Gap.
- A continuation lower on EUR/USD is favored, aligning with a continuation higher on the DXY.

- INSTITUTIONAL ORDER FLOW AND SELL SIDE TARGETS
Analyzing the EUR/USD daily chart, noting the precision at the weekly volume imbalance.
- A small 'Mohawk' above the level is an expected deviation outside the range defined for an inefficiency
being repriced.
- Price broke lower, creating a Breakaway Gap.
- The market traded up to both 'Institutional Order Flow Entry Drills'. These drills involve trading into a Fair
Value Gap but not even reaching the midpoint (consequent encroachment), implying only a partial entry
before aggressive movement.
- Price traded aggressively lower, back into the weekly inversion Fair Value Gap, then consolidated through
August.
- It traded back up to the last up-close candle before displacement (bearish order block), then moved back
down into the weekly inversion FVG.
- Sell side liquidity resides below here and below here.

- SIBI AND BALANCED PRICE RANGE
On the EUR/USD four-hour chart, the Institutional Order Flow Entry Drill from the daily chart is visible.
- The market broke aggressively lower, creating a 'sell side imbalance buy side inefficiency' (SIBI).
- Price repriced back into the SIBI, consolidated, then broke lower, trading down into the weekly inversion
Fair Value Gap.
- It traded to the high of the inversion FVG as support, then traded back down, swiping the sell side.
- Distribution occurred to relative equal highs where buy side liquidity would be resting.
- A bearish order block traded down to a SIBI, but more specifically, a Balanced Price Range.
- Price treated it as such, then traded aggressively back into the weekly inversion FVG.
- The focus going forward is over here, assuming DXY continues moving higher.

- RELATIVE EQUAL HIGHS AND DOWNSIDE TARGETS
Examining the EUR/USD hourly chart, price traded up into the bearish order block.
- Multiple relative equal highs were identified, which was tweeted earlier in the week as "too obvious."
- Price did rally up into that area, and once it got through the bearish order block and rejected, everything
started moving lower with a higher dollar.
- An Institutional Order Flow Entry Drill is observed with a SIBI, where one candle penetrated the Fair
Value Gap (between a candle's low and another's high) with a small, partial entry, followed by an
aggressive sell-off.
- An up-close candle prior to the discount range of the weekly inversion FVG acted as a bearish order block
when traded into, leading to lower prices.
- Price is now consolidating. Keep an eye on the current low, other lows, and the low of the weekly
inversion Fair Value Gap.
- If price animates to the downside (supported by a bullish dollar), the attention is drawn below the 1.0835
- ish level. Lack of bullish order flow on DXY would make this difficult.

- S&P INEFFICIENCY & AUGUST TRADING CONCERNS
Moving to the weekly charts of E-mini S&P (ES) and NASDAQ futures (September 2023 contracts).
- On the S&P, a rally turned into an inefficiency, which has been monitored (refer to previous market
reviews).
- A break lower would trade below a specific gap, which would normally be a bullish discount FVG, but not
after reaching a higher timeframe weekly inefficiency and seeing this type of breakdown.
- Seasonally, August is a fickle month; traders are advised to take a vacation from pushing hard, especially
with funded account challenges or high leverage trades, as it can be a "meat grinder."
- Many on social media have complained about getting "beat up and chopped up" in August. Historically,
August is often a month to stand aside or be very nimble for day/short-term traders.

- PROPULSION BLOCKS AND NASDAQ WEAKNESS
Continued analysis of indices weekly charts, starting with the S&P Propulsion Block.
- A down-close candle acts as an order block, followed by another down-close candle (propulsion block)
that digs into the first one, but only to about halfway.
- If price closes below half of the propulsion block (an order block that digs into a previous order block), it
becomes extremely bearish, indicating an abandonment of bullishness and anticipation of lower prices.
- While price did not trade to the mean threshold of this propulsion block, the speaker personally believes
price will dig into the sell side next week.
- For more on propulsion blocks, refer to Month 4 content of the mentorship core lessons and the PDRA
Matrix.
- On the NASDAQ weekly chart, the structure of down-close candles forming an order block and propulsion
block is less clean than the S&P, going a little past midpoint, making it more prone to fail, and indeed price
dug through it.
- NASDAQ has been weaker, now leading to the downside, a shift from recent months where it was the
upside performer.

- RELATIVE WEAKNESS AND SHORT BIAS
Further details on NASDAQ's price action.
- The last up-close candle prior to displacement is a bearish order block, which price traded up into this
week.
- Two weeks ago's weekly low was traded up into, followed by 'Power of Three' (sell side delivery) attacking
the short-term low this week.
- The S&P did not exhibit the same measure of running the sell side, indicating a reluctance to go as low
and as fast as NASDAQ.
- This characteristic or signature suggests that bears would be better suited to trade short on the NASDAQ,
which aligns with the speaker's majority of trades, including live examples.
- The expectation is for a run to sell side liquidity, a potential draw on liquidity over the next week or two if
animation occurs.
- 'Buy Side Imbalance Sell Side Efficiency' (BIBS) or Fair Value Gaps are present lower down, but
NASDAQ is favored to reach them faster and sooner than the S&P.

- SMT DIVERGENCE AND MARKET STRUCTURE SHIFT
Analyzing the daily charts for E-mini S&P and NASDAQ.
- Immediately note the SMT Divergence: compare candle highs, observing how NASDAQ was unwilling to
make a higher high compared to the S&P, indicating a cracking correlation.
- An imbalance was traded into, where price spent time before finally breaking away.
- This volume imbalance can act much like a breakaway gap (more details reserved for upcoming books).
- Price moved from repricing a 'sell side imbalance buy side efficiency' (SIBI), trading up to the last up
- close candle (labeled as a bearish order block through a lower timeframe lens).
- This area serves as both the high of an inefficiency and an order block on lower timeframes.
- Even if it's a down-close candle, the highest one can be an up-close candle that forms a bearish order
block on lower timeframes.
- The market broke lower, taking out the short-term low, signaling a 'shift in market structure'
- a Model
2022 setup.
- This includes a bearish order block (last up-close candle), a breaker (high-low-higher), and price trading
up into it before breaking lower.
- An up-close candle during a 'sell program' is very influential; its mean threshold and opening price are
highly sensitive levels to study.
- Price traded up into these levels, followed by an aggressive move lower, taking out the sell side.

- MEAN THRESHOLD AND PREMIUM WICK ENCROACHMENT
Continuing the daily chart comparison, noting the lack of a comparable sell side run on the S&P.
- On the S&P, a 'Buy Side Imbalance Sell Side Efficiency' (BIBS) is present, which is a Fair Value Gap,
representing a discount.
- Its consequent encroachment (half of an inefficiency or gap, or half of an order block, also known as
mean threshold) is shown.
- Observe the up-close candle: its mean threshold is half of its range. Displacement occurred after trading
into this.
- On the NASDAQ, there wasn't a run up in respect of the mean threshold of the bullish candle; instead, it
ran all the way back up to the premium wick consequent encroachment.
- Wicks are treated just like gaps, meaning their halfway form or midpoint would be considered consequent
encroachment.

- UNUSUAL PRICE ACTION & TWITTER INSIGHTS
Moving to the four-hour charts for ES and NASDAQ.
- On the S&P, a bearish order block was traded into, repricing an inefficiency (Fair Value Gap as a 'sell side
imbalance buy side efficiency'
- SIBI).
- A strong displacement lower took out a low, and price traded back up.
- The subsequent price action was highly unusual, with low, high, low, high, low, high, low, higher high, low,
choppy movement
- something rarely seen in 30 years (only 5-6 times).
- This type of market requires nimbleness, contrasting with many social media influencers who were "hard
- pressed" to go short, often arguing against bearish stances.
- This context contributed to the speaker's primary focus on the short side this week.
- A specific Fair Value Gap was highlighted in a short video on Twitter, which price traded down into today.
- Following on Twitter provides timely, "guerrilla warfare tactics" insights, especially before November 2023,
when Twitter activity will cease.

- BREAKERS AND CHOPPY DELIVERY
Further four-hour analysis, focusing on the bodies of the candles which tell the narrative.
- On the S&P, a breaker (low-high-lower low) was identified. Price went through it, heading to the
consequent encroachment of a level at 4459.25 (validated by the candle's low at 4459.00).
- On NASDAQ, a bearish order block was traded into, then back up into mean threshold, before trading
lower.
- A 'sell side imbalance buy side inefficiency' (SIBI) was repriced back into, again at its mean threshold.
- Transposing daily and weekly annotations to lower timeframes helps maintain perspective.
- The market traded aggressively down into the sell side, as mentioned in the context of the four-hour Fair
Value Gap.
- While price delivered aggressively lower, it featured a lot of back and forth, choppy action, which some
might retrospectively label as a "descending trading range."
- The challenge is trading this before it happens, as the speaker aims to outline future market structure
rather than just hindsight observations.

- LIQUIDITY RUNS AND FVG TARGETS
Moving to the hourly charts for ES and NASDAQ.
- On the S&P, buy side liquidity and a premium wick consequent encroachment were targeted, with price
aggressively breaking lower after trading into it.
- Friday saw significant back and forth price action, digging into the four-hour Fair Value Gap and its
consequent encroachment (blue shaded area).
- Notably, every day, the previous day's high was taken out, running buy side liquidity, preventing short-term
traders from staying in positions for the rest of the week.
- Thursday saw a run up followed by an aggressive sell-off, and Friday, while "junky," was certainly tradable.
- On NASDAQ, a similar pattern with a 'sell side imbalance buy side inefficiency' (SIBI) was seen, traded
up into, taking buy side.
- Relative equal lows (sell side) were residing below, and the market traded through that, then back up into
the mean threshold of a bullish order block on the higher timeframe.
- Price broke lower, took sell side, then came all the way back up on Thursday to 'blow out' the high on the
9th (buy side liquidity).
- It then eroded, created relative equal lows, came back up in consequent encroachment of a wick, then
sold off.
- A Fair Value Gap was traded up into, broke lower, consolidated, and as outlined in a Twitter commentary,
price animated down into the four-hour Fair Value Gap and a specific sell side liquidity level, though it didn't
go as deep into the FVG as desired.

- AUGUST MARKET CONDITIONS
Closing with observations on the 15-minute charts for both ES and NASDAQ, which showed sloppy
consolidation.
- The remaining weeks of August are expected to present some of the most unfavorable price action.
- Even if clean price delivery appears, it's not normal for August. The speaker's warnings about caution are
generally accurate, sparing listeners from difficulties.
- August is typically a month where the market is just getting ready for the clearer price action of the fall
months (September, October, November, possibly early December).
- These fall months, along with springtime, are considered the "sweetest times of the year to trade."
- It's advised not to "waste gunpowder" in the next couple of weeks in August; instead, relax and let the
markets do their choppy thing.

- PATIENCE, PREPARATION, AND FUTURE OUTLOOK
Elaborating on the August market behavior and strategy.
- The choppy price action seen in August is precisely what the speaker warned about; while targets were
hit, the delivery was not easy to trade.
- If you felt like it was harder, this experience is valuable, helping you appreciate the warnings better.
- If you've incurred drawdowns, blown account challenges, or are pursuing a funded account, do not rush to
restart.
- Wait until September. Ignore the temptation to jump back in now.
- Traditionally, August is a "sloppy" type of month due to big money being on the sidelines, leading to this
type of unpredictable action.
- While it's tradable, it will likely be stressful, potentially leading to more losses than in better months, and
could cause impulsiveness.
- Use August for prep work: define goals for the fall and year-end, assess growth, refine organization in
annotations and timeframe management.
- Go through notes from videos, revisit them, and study, but relax.
- September will bring better market conditions with more volatility, offering better participation
opportunities.
